title: ETA Reliability Index â€” USâ€“West Africa Ro-Ro Lane, Q1 2026
summary: Q1 2026 ETA Reliability Index for the USâ€“West Africa ro-ro lane. Overall on-time rate: 68% (within 48h of declared ETA). Sallaum: 78%. Grimaldi: 58%. Primary delay drivers: Lagos port congestion, Bight of Benin weather, vessel engineering holds.
body: ETA Reliability Index â€” USâ€“West Africa Ro-Ro Lane, Q1 2026. Shipping Lane Analyst publishes quarterly ETA reliability data for major ro-ro shipping corridors. This edition covers the US East Coast to West Africa (Nigeria) lane for Q1 2026 (Januaryâ€“March). METHODOLOGY: 'On time' is defined as arriving at Lagos berth within 48 hours of the ETA declared at the time of cargo booking. Data sourced from AIS tracking, carrier bulletins, and port agent reports. Confidence in data: High (6 observed voyages in period). OVERALL LANE PERFORMANCE: Combined on-time rate: 68% (within 48h). Average delay when late: 2.8 days beyond declared ETA. CARRIER BREAKDOWN: Sallaum Lines (WA-1 service): On-time rate 78% (5 voyages, 1 delay >48h). Average delay when late: 2 days. Average anchor wait at Lagos: 4.2 days. Grimaldi Lines (WAX service): On-time rate 58% (4 voyages, 2 delays >48h). Average delay when late: 3.2 days. Average anchor wait at Apapa/Tin Can: 3.8 days. DELAY ATTRIBUTION: Lagos port congestion (beyond carrier control): 65% of delays. Bight of Benin weather: 20% of delays. Carrier-side issues (engineering, schedule change): 15% of delays. TREND: Q1 2026 on-time rate (68%) is slightly below Q1 2025 (74%) and Q4 2025 (71%), reflecting elevated Lagos congestion. RECOMMENDATION: Importers on this lane should budget 3 additional days beyond carrier-declared ETA as a planning buffer, and 7 additional days beyond vessel ETA for realistic vehicle delivery from terminal.

title: Ro-Ro Freight Rates â€” US to West Africa, Q1 2026 Market Update
summary: Ro-ro freight rate market update, USâ€“West Africa, Q1 2026. Passenger vehicles: $850â€“$1,200 per unit. SUVs/pickups: $1,000â€“$1,500. Commercial vehicles: $1,200â€“$2,000. Rates up 8% vs Q1 2025 driven by increased demand and congestion surcharges.
body: Ro-Ro Freight Rates â€” US to West Africa Q1 2026. Shipping Lane Analyst publishes quarterly freight rate intelligence for key ro-ro corridors. RATE RANGES (Q1 2026, US East Coast to Lagos): Compact/Sedan vehicles (up to 4.5m): $850â€“$1,200 per unit. SUVs/Pickup trucks (4.5mâ€“5.5m): $1,000â€“$1,500 per unit. Large SUVs/Minivans (5.5mâ€“6.0m): $1,200â€“$1,600 per unit. Commercial vehicles/trucks: $1,200â€“$2,000 per unit, depending on dimensions and weight. SURCHARGES CURRENTLY IN EFFECT (Q1 2026): Port Congestion Surcharge (PCS): $75â€“$150 per unit. This surcharge was introduced by both Sallaum and Grimaldi in Q4 2025 to offset the cost of extended Lagos anchorage time. Bunker Adjustment Factor (BAF): $60â€“$100 per unit. Fuel cost has moderated but remains above 2023 levels. RATE TREND: Q1 2026 rates are approximately 8% above Q1 2025. The increase reflects: (1) demand growth (Nigerian vehicle imports up ~10% YoY), (2) the new Lagos congestion surcharge, (3) higher terminal handling charges after NPA 15% increase. SALLAUM vs GRIMALDI PRICING: Rates are broadly comparable between the two carriers. Grimaldi is typically $50â€“$100 cheaper per unit but has lower on-time reliability on this lane. Sallaum commands a modest premium for its higher service reliability. OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 rates expected to be broadly stable. The Sallaum bi-weekly service announcement (June 2026) may put modest downward pressure on rates H2 2026.

title: Nigeria Vehicle Import Market â€” Demand Drivers and Outlook 2026
summary: Analysis of Nigeria vehicle import demand drivers for 2026. Annual import volume estimated at 350,000â€“400,000 units. Key drivers: private sector growth, FX improvement, age restriction enforcement. Outlook: steady growth despite FX headwinds.
body: Nigeria Vehicle Import Market â€” Demand Drivers and Outlook 2026. Nigeria is Sub-Saharan Africa's largest vehicle import market. This analysis covers the demand dynamics for the ro-ro corridor. MARKET SIZE: Estimated annual vehicle imports into Nigeria: 350,000â€“400,000 units (2025 actuals). Approximately 60% by sea (ro-ro and container), 40% via land borders from Cotonou, Benin. Ro-ro share of sea imports: growing, now approximately 35% of sea imports. DEMAND DRIVERS: (1) PRIVATE SECTOR GROWTH: Nigeria's private sector vehicle fleet is aging. Average age of vehicles on Nigerian roads: 15+ years. Replacement demand is structural and ongoing. (2) FX IMPROVEMENT: The CBN's managed float policy since 2023 has improved formal FX access, reducing the premium on the parallel market and making legal vehicle imports more predictable. (3) AGE RESTRICTION ENFORCEMENT: NCS has tightened enforcement of the 15-year import prohibition. This is pushing buyers toward newer (and more expensive) vehicles, supporting per-unit values and freight revenue. (4) GOVERNMENT PROCUREMENT: Federal and state government vehicle procurement programmes add lumpy but significant demand. (5) CORPORATE FLEETS: Nigerian multinational corporations are renewing fleets after the 2023â€“2024 economic downturn. RISK FACTORS: Naira depreciation adds cost for importers. If USD/NGN reaches 1,800, some marginal demand may price out. Fuel availability and infrastructure constraints limit vehicle utility in some markets. OUTLOOK: Moderate growth of 5â€“10% in ro-ro import volumes expected in 2026. The Sallaum bi-weekly service announcement validates the market opportunity.

title: Which Is Safer for Vehicle Imports â€” Apapa or Tin Can? A Comparative Guide
summary: Practical comparison of Apapa Quays vs Tin Can Island for vehicle importers. Tin Can rated higher on cargo security and clearance speed. Apapa has better road access and more customs agents. Both have similar congestion levels in 2026.
body: Apapa Quays vs Tin Can Island Terminal â€” Comparative Guide for Vehicle Importers 2026. OVERVIEW: Lagos has two main terminals for ro-ro vehicle imports: Apapa Quays (NPA-operated) and Tin Can Island Terminal (also NPA, with private terminal management). The choice between them is usually determined by your carrier â€” Sallaum predominantly uses Tin Can, Grimaldi uses Apapa. However, understanding the differences helps in planning. CARGO SECURITY: Tin Can Island: CCTV coverage across 95% of yard. Perimeter fencing and security checkpoints. Lower reported vehicle damage rate: 1.2% of consignments in Q1 2026. Apapa Quays: Older facility, fewer cameras. Higher reported vehicle damage rate: 2.1% of consignments. Apapa has a higher rate of reported vehicle theft/tampering attempts (though absolute numbers remain low). RATING: Tin Can is generally safer for cargo. CUSTOMS CLEARANCE SPEED: Tin Can Island: Average clearance: 6.2 working days (Q1 2026). NCS at Tin Can uses Risk Management System (RMS) â€” 35% of consignments selected for physical examination. Apapa Quays: Average clearance: 5.8 working days. NCS at Apapa: 60% selected for physical examination, but examinations are often faster (larger NCS team at Apapa). RATING: Comparable, slight edge to Apapa for total throughput speed. ROAD ACCESS: Apapa has better direct road connections (though both are congested). Tin Can requires crossing the Tin Can Island bridge â€” which floods during heavy rain. RATING: Apapa has better road access. RECOMMENDATION: For cargo security, prefer Tin Can. For road access, prefer Apapa. On balance, both are acceptable if you have a trusted clearing agent at the relevant terminal.

title: Port Congestion Seasonality â€” Lagos Ro-Ro Anchor Wait Times by Month (2023â€“2025)
summary: Historical average anchorage wait times for ro-ro vessels at Lagos by month, 2023â€“2025. Peak months: Marchâ€“April (pre-Easter, 5â€“7 days average) and Novemberâ€“December (holiday surge, 6â€“8 days). Low season: Julyâ€“August (2â€“3 days).
body: Lagos Ro-Ro Anchorage Wait Seasonality â€” Historical Data 2023â€“2025. Shipping Lane Analyst has compiled historical data on anchorage wait times for ro-ro vessels at Lagos (combined Tin Can Island and Apapa) by month for 2023â€“2025. AVERAGE ANCHOR WAIT BY MONTH (days, 2023â€“2025 average): January: 3.2 days. February: 3.8 days. March: 5.1 days. April: 6.8 days (Easter surge peak). May: 4.5 days. June: 3.8 days. July: 2.8 days (low season). August: 2.4 days (lowest congestion of year). September: 3.2 days. October: 4.0 days. November: 5.8 days. December: 7.2 days (Christmas/year-end peak). YEAR-ON-YEAR TREND: All months in 2025 showed higher anchor wait times than 2023, reflecting the structural growth in Lagos import volumes. 2026 Q1 data (Januaryâ€“March) shows a further 0.8-day increase on average vs 2025 equivalent months. IMPLICATIONS FOR CURRENT SHIPMENTS: March 2026 average anchor wait: estimated 5.5â€“6.5 days (above the 5.1-day 3-year average). April 2026 vessels (GREAT ABIDJAN, GREAT COTONOU) are arriving during the peak Easter congestion window. Apply the 6.8-day April average as a planning estimate, with a range of 5â€“9 days. PLANNING GUIDANCE: For downstream logistics scheduling (customs agents, trucking, warehouse), use the following rule of thumb: Add anchor wait estimate + 2 days discharge + 6 days NCS clearance = total days from vessel Lagos arrival to vehicle at your location. For April 2026 arrivals: 7 + 2 + 6 = 15 days from vessel arrival to vehicle collection. Best case (low congestion, clean docs): 10 days. Worst case (high congestion, exam required): 20+ days.
